articles.title: Country diary: The most magical of frost-coated mornings | Mark Cocker
articles.description: Knotbury, Staffordshire: A truly special dawn, where last night’s ice lingered on everything, and I was joined by no fewer than six ring ouzels As I drove to this tiny moorland hamlet, the dawn sky looked so grey that I imagined it must have 100% cloud cover. Actually, there was none, and as the blue slowly crept in overhead, I could see that frost was everywhere. I also realised that there was no breeze and every sound seemed distilled, so I stopped by the first farm to record my blackbird. He has mastered the sweetest imitations of displaying golden plovers, but this was my first chance to capture them. And there he was, doing his plover notes, but throwing in snippets of curlew as extras, and when he stood in profile at the roof apex, singing, bill wide, throat feathers spiked against the heavens, I knew the morning would be magical. Continue reading...

articles.title: Almost half of EU’s busiest flight routes are ‘hard or impossible’ to book on trains – report
articles.description: ‘Stone age’ system of booking cross-border rail tickets holding back climate action by consumers, says thinktank Europe’s “stone age” system of booking train tickets makes it needlessly difficult for travellers to avoid polluting flights, a report has found. Booking equivalent train tickets is “difficult or impossible” on almost half of the EU’s busiest international air routes, analysis from the Transport & Environment (T&E) thinktank shows. Continue reading...

articles.title: On the trail with the hunters who believe shooting big game can save Africa’s wildlife
articles.description: One way to pay for wildlife conservation is to allow the rich to bag a few animals for high prices. But critics see this approach as an exercise in neocolonialism You can kill almost anything if you’re willing to pay. Big or small. Land, water or air. Ten a penny or one of the last of its kind. There’s nearly always a way, though it might not make you popular. The Niassa special reserve, a vast reservation larger than Switzerland, stretches for 190 miles along the northern rim of Mozambique, taking in 4.2m hectares of woodland and rivers. The reserve, one of the world’s largest protected areas, is home to elephants, leopards, hyenas, zebras and about 1,000 wild lions. That word, however: protected. It applies to some, but not all, of its animal inhabitants. Each year, a specific number are set aside for sacrifice, for the greater good. Not long ago, I joined an expedition in Niassa, with one of Africa’s top game-hunting companies. Continue reading...

articles.title: HEAT INDEX ALERT: Cavite expected to reach ‘danger’ level Tuesday

Heat index refers to the “feels like” temperature, or how hot it actually feels to the human body when air temperature is combined with humidity, the Philippine Atmospheric, Geophysical and Astronomical Services Administration (Pagasa) said. articles.title: Hospitals in Cebu on heightened alert for health effects tied to haze
articles.description: CEBU CITY, Philippines – Although no one has been hospitalized due to the haze so far, hospitals in Metro Cebu have been urged to prepare. These were among the measures the provincial government has started implementing as Cebu Province’s urban center remains blanketed with smog for a third straight day on Monday, April 20. Gov. Pamela Baricuatro instructed that Capitol-run hospitals, particularly in Metro Cebu, be placed on heightened alert, said Ainjeliz Dela Torre-Orong, provincial information officer (PIO). articles.title: Dangerous heat index grips 3 areas on Monday
articles.description: MANILA, Philippines — Three areas experienced a dangerous heat index on Monday, according tothe state weather bureau. The Philippine Atmospheric, Geophysical, and Astronomical Services Administration (Pagasa) reported that as of 5 p.m., Sangley Point, Cavite logged the highest peak heat index for the day at 43oC. This marked the second consecutive day Sangley Point recorded a danger-level heat index after peaking at 44oC on Sunday. Meanwhile, Baler, Aurora and Iloilo City both had a heat index of 42oC.
